title: Testing inks for calligraphy, flow, dry time, and lightfastness
subtitle: How to evaluate any ink before committing it to finished work
excerpt: Not all inks behave in a broad-edge or pointed pen. Fountain-pen inks are often too thin; acrylic inks clog nibs; some India inks dry to a surface that breaks with the paper. A fifteen-minute test battery identifies the problems before they appear on a finished piece.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on inks: Johnston's analysis of lamp-black ink, iron-gall ink, and various commercial inks of his day forms the basis of the criteria used here, updated to reflect modern ink categories. Johnston's writing is in the public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dominant ink of the medieval and early modern period, made from oak galls (a tannin source) reacted with iron sulphate. Starts blue-black, ages to a warm brown. Slightly corrosive to paper over centuries but historically the most lightfast of all inks.
Resistance to fading under light exposure. Pigment-based inks are generally more lightfast than dye-based inks.

content.text: There are four ink categories in common calligraphy use:
, carbon/pigment inks, dye inks (fountain pen style), and acrylic inks. Each category has different flow characteristics, dry times, nib compatibility, and
. Knowing which category you are working with, and what to expect from it, saves ruined pieces.

Load the nib and hold it at a 45° angle over a piece of cartridge paper without touching the paper. A good ink flows freely by gravity within three seconds. If the ink does not flow freely, if it beads on the nib or needs the nib to touch the paper to start, it is too viscous for that nib size. Dilute with distilled water, a few drops at a time, until it flows. Very thin inks (most fountain-pen inks) flow too freely and feather on cartridge paper, test on hot-press watercolour paper, which is more resistant.

After writing several lines, rinse the nib in clean water and inspect it. An ink that leaves a significant residue after rinsing, a dark ring around the breather hole or a sticky film on the tines, may clog the nib in extended use. Shellac-based inks in particular dry hard and fast; they must be cleaned from the nib within thirty minutes or risk permanent damage. Acrylic inks behave similarly.

Write a short test phrase. Allow it to dry for two hours. Then drag a wet brush or damp sponge across the dried text. A water-resistant ink (carbon pigment, shellac-based) survives this without smearing. A dye ink dissolves immediately. A gum-based iron-gall ink may re-dissolve slightly but the carbon particles remain. Important if the work will be combined with washes or watercolour.

Cut a test strip in half. Cover one half with black card and leave the other in a south-facing window for two weeks. Compare. Dye inks fade visibly in two weeks. Carbon pigment inks are unchanged. Iron-gall inks shift colour (from blue-black to warm brown) but retain density. If the ink will be framed and displayed, only use inks that pass a two-week fade test without visible change on the exposed half.

Iron-gall ink: the first choice for archival broad-edge work on paper or vellum. Sumi ink: the first choice for East Asian-tradition broad-edge work and suminagashi. Carbon pigment inks (not shellac-based): good all-rounders for broad-edge and pointed pen, water-resistant when dry. Acrylic inks: suited to pointed pen on smooth surfaces for colour work, but require disciplined nib cleaning. Dye inks: practice only, not lightfast enough for finished pieces.
